title: Trees Glow With Electricity During Storms—And Scientists Just Caught It on Camera
summary:
For more than half a century, scientists have suspected that forests might secretly glow during thunderstorms. But proving it? That took a road trip, a modified minivan, and a lot of patience.

In June 2024, a team from Penn State University set out in a 2013 Toyota Sienna equipped with a custom-built telescopic weather instrument. Their mission: to capture a phenomenon called "corona discharge"—tiny bursts of electricity forming at the tips of leaves during storms. These sparks produce a faint ultraviolet (UV) glow, but no one had ever confirmed them in a natural setting.

The team, led by doctoral student Patrick McFarland and distinguished professor William Brune, headed to Florida, hoping to catch its near-daily summer thunderstorms. But for three weeks, the storms fizzled out before they could collect useful data.

As they headed back to Pennsylvania, conditions changed. Large, long-lasting storms developed just west of Interstate 95. The researchers stopped at the University of North Carolina at Pembroke, set up their equipment in a parking lot, and aimed it at a sweetgum tree about 100 feet away.

A thunderstorm persisted for nearly two hours, bringing heavy rain and frequent lightning. During that time, the team recorded 859 corona events on the sweetgum tree and 93 on a nearby loblolly pine. Each event lasted from a fraction of a second to several seconds.

"This just goes to show that there's still discovery science being done," said McFarland, lead author of the paper published in Geophysical Research Letters. "For more than half a century, scientists have theorized that corona exists, but this proves it."

How does it work? Thunderclouds develop large negative charges that attract positive charges on the ground. That positive charge travels upward through trees, concentrating at the highest points—leaf tips. The electric field becomes intense enough to produce a faint glow.

Here's the really cool part: these sparks help clean the air. The UV radiation from corona discharges breaks apart water vapor molecules, forming hydroxyl—the atmosphere's primary oxidizer. Hydroxyl reacts with pollutants (including methane, a potent greenhouse gas) and transforms them into substances that are easier to eliminate.

Using their Corona Observing Telescope System—a Newtonian telescope connected to a UV-sensitive camera—the team captured 859 events on the sweetgum and 93 on the pine. Additional observations were made during four other storms across four different tree species.

"There's still discovery science being done," McFarland said. And sometimes, all it takes is a van, a telescope, and a good storm.
why_it_matters: This discovery not only confirms a long-standing scientific hypothesis but also reveals a natural air-cleaning process that could inform climate models and new technologies for pollution control—directly affecting the future environment that today's teens will inherit.

keywords.explanation: Tiny electrical bursts at leaf tips during storms that produce UV light
The tree on which 859 corona events were recorded using a telescope system
Invisible light used by the camera to capture the faint glow of the sparks
A molecule formed from water vapor by UV radiation; it breaks down pollutants like methane
A potent greenhouse gas that hydroxyl helps remove from the atmosphere
A thunderstorm that lasted nearly two hours, providing conditions for the observations

options: A custom-built Corona Observing Telescope System
questions.correct_answer: A custom-built Corona Observing Telescope System

questions.question: How many corona events were recorded on the sweetgum tree?
Where did the team finally make their successful observation?
options: University of North Carolina at Pembroke
questions.correct_answer: University of North Carolina at Pembroke

questions.question: What does the UV radiation from corona discharges create from water vapor?

background_read: Corona discharge is a well-known phenomenon in electrical engineering, often seen as a bluish glow around high-voltage power lines, but it was only theorized to occur naturally on trees during thunderstorms.
Hydroxyl (OH) is called the 'detergent of the atmosphere' because it reacts with pollutants, including methane and volatile organic compounds, breaking them down and helping to clean the air.
Methane is a greenhouse gas about 25 times more potent than carbon dioxide over 100 years; natural removal by hydroxyl is a critical part of the global methane cycle.
The Penn State team built their telescope system inside a minivan, allowing them to chase storms and set up quickly in parking lots—a creative solution for field research.
Article_Structure: LEAD: 'For more than half a century, scientists have suspected that forests might secretly glow during thunderstorms.'
├─ Team sets out in June 2024 with modified van and telescope
├─ First destination: Florida, but storms fizzle for 3 weeks
└─ On return trip, large storms develop west of I-95
├─ Stop at UNC Pembroke parking lot
├─ Aim at sweetgum tree 100 feet away
└─ Record 859 corona events on sweetgum, 93 on loblolly pine
├─ Negative charge in clouds draws positive charge upward through trees
└─ Electric field at leaf tips causes corona discharge (UV glow)
├─ UV breaks water vapor into hydroxyl
└─ Hydroxyl breaks down pollutants, including methane
CONCLUSION: A van, a telescope, and a good storm proved a 70-year theory

perspectives.description: The observation is a triumph of field research and ingenuity, proving a 70-year-old theory and opening up an entirely new area of study about the role of trees in atmospheric chemistry.

While exciting, the study only captured a few hundred events on two trees during one storm. It is still unclear how much corona discharge contributes to overall atmospheric hydroxyl production compared to other sources like sunlight.

The successful method—a mobile telescope system in a van—demonstrates how creative instrumentation can solve long-standing observational challenges, and it sets a precedent for future 'storm chasing' research in other fields.

If future research shows that tree corona discharges produce significant amounts of hydroxyl, efforts to preserve and plant more trees might be seen not only as carbon sinks but also as natural air purifiers that help fight climate change and pollution.
